analogy: Think of "Nutrition in Animals" through the chapter's own opening images: Nutrition in Animals 2 Y ou have learnt in Chapter 1 that plants can prepare their own food by the process of photosynthesis but animals cannot. Animals get their food from plants, either directly by eating plants or indirectly by eating animals that eat plants.... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 2.1 DIFFERENT WAYS OF TAKING FOOD, SCIENCE 12, 2.2 DIGESTION IN HUMANS, N UTRITION IN ANIMALS 13. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Nutrition in Animals is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that nutrition in Animals 2 Y ou have learnt in Chapter 1 that plants can prepare their own food by the process of photosynthesis but animals cannot.

The text explains that animals get their food from plants, either directly by eating plants or indirectly by eating animals that eat plants.

The text explains that recall that all organisms including humans require food for growth, repair and functioning of the body.

The text explains that animal nutrition includes nutrient requirement, mode of intake of food and its utilisation in the body.

The text explains that you have studied in Class VI that food consists of many components.

The text explains that try to recall and list them below: 1. ______________________ 2. ______________________ 3. ______________________ 4. ______________________ 5. ______________________ 6. ______________________ The components of food such as carbohydrates are complex substances.

The text explains that these complex substances cannot be utilised as such.

The text explains that so they are broken down into simpler substances.

The text explains that the breakdown of complex components of Table 2.1 Various modes of feeding Name of Kind of Mode of animal food feeding Snail Ant Eagle Humming-bird Lice Mosquito Butterfly House fly (Scraping, chewing, siphoning, capturing and swallowing, sponging, sucking etc.) food into simpler substances is called digestion.

The text explains that 2.1 DIFFERENT WAYS OF TAKING FOOD The mode of taking food into the body varies in different organisms.

The text explains that bees and humming-birds suck the nectar of plants, infants of human and many other animals feed on mother’s milk.

The text explains that snakes like the python swallow the animals they prey upon.

The text explains that some aquatic animals filter tiny food particles floating nearby and feed upon them.

The text explains that write down your observations in the given Table.

The text explains that you may find the list of modes of feeding given below the Table helpful.

The text explains that 2.1 Starfish Amazing fact Starfish feeds on animals covered by hard shells of calcium carbonate.

The text explains that the digestive tract and the associated glands together constitute the digestive system.

The text explains that 2.2 Human digestive system Buccal cavity Salivary gland Stomach Pancreas Small intestine Large intestine Rectum Anus Gall bladder Liver Oesophagus

The text explains that just imagine how such a long structure is accommodated in a small space within our body!

The text explains that these bacteria break down the sugars present from the leftover food and release acids (see Chapter 4 to know what an acid is).

The text explains that it is free at the front and can be moved in all directions.

The text explains that n UTRITION IN ANIMALS 15 Paheli wants to know how food moves in the opposite direction during vomiting.

The text explains that recall the instances when you vomited after eating and think of the reason for it.

The text explains that the liver is a reddish brown gland situated in the upper part of the abdomen on the right side.

The text explains that 2.9 Digestive system of ruminant Boojho wants to know why we cannot digest cellulose like the cattle do.

The text explains that animals like horses, rabbit, etc., have a large sac-like structure called Caecum between the oesophagus and the small intestine (Fig.

The text explains that so far you have learnt about animals which possess the digestive system.

The text explains that but there are many small organisms which do not have a mouth and a digestive system.

The text explains that this process is called rumination and these animals are called ruminants.

The text explains that the basic process of digestion of food and release of energy is the same in all animals.

The text explains that in a later chapter you will learn about the transport of food absorbed by the intestine to the various parts of the body.

The text explains that what you have learnt  Animal nutrition includes nutrient requirement, mode of intake of food and its utilisation in the body.  The human digestive system consists of the alimentary canal and secretory glands.

The text explains that the stomach wall and the wall of the small intestine also secrete digestive juices.  The modes of feeding vary in different organisms.  Nutrition is a complex process involving: (i) ingestion, (ii) digestion, (iii) absorption, (iv) assimilation and (v) egestion.

The text explains that you may take help of a doctor, a dietician, your teacher or any other person, or from any other source.

The text explains that (iii) How does glucose help the patient recover?

The text explains that find out what vitamins are and get the following information.

The text explains that (ii) Which fruits or vegetables should be eaten regularly to get vitamins?

The text explains that write a one-page note on the information collected by you.

The text explains that collect data from your friends, neighbours and classmates to know more about “milk teeth”.

The text explains that age at which Age at which No. of teeth No. of teeth first tooth fell last tooth fell lost replaced 1.

The text explains that find out from at least twenty children and find the average age at which children lose the milk teeth.

The text explains that fats in goat’s milk are much simpler than those in cow’s milk.

The text explains that therefore, the goat’s milk is much easier to digest than the cow’s milk.

Source: NCERT Class 7 Science, Chapter 2: Nutrition in Animals

prelims_traps: Do not treat 2.1 DIFFERENT WAYS OF TAKING FOOD and SCIENCE 12 as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Nutrition in Animals; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Nutrition in Animals through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 2.1 DIFFERENT WAYS OF TAKING FOOD and SCIENCE 12 to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
